rows.query_text: What is this archive for?
rows.model_prompt:
You are running a research-only browser-local RAG experiment.
Generated text is not archive evidence.
Do not output hidden reasoning, chain-of-thought, or <think> tags.

Question: What is this archive for?
Intent: archive_orientation
Required behavior: answer about the archive view as a whole, not about one object.
Keep the generated answer body under 45 words.
Start the answer body with: Based on these records,

Archive facts you may use:
- This is a source-linked research archive for graphic design and visual communication records.
- It organizes records through folders/topology: Unresolved region, Midcentury modern graphic communication, Trade card, Research lab method, India, Graphic design object record.
- Surface types in the packet: sheet, method.
- Publication roles in the packet: support_packet_appendix_text, method_context, main_sheet.
- Source families represented: Library of Congress loc.gov API, browser-local-rag-lab method files.
- Rights states represented: rights_review_required, research_fixture, thumbnail_candidate.
- Image-state codes represented: IMG04, IMG00, IMG01.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
Write only the concise natural-language answer body.
Do not write the official evidence tag block or field labels; the browser lab appends exact tags after generation.
Do not name a single record as the archive.

rights.label: Research-only method fixture; not archive object evidence.

method_context.evidence_definition: Use source-linked metadata, compact text, source, rights, image-state, and topology fields as retrieval evidence.
method_context.answer_lane_policy: Choose help, fast_answer, source_rights, research_answer, or refusal_more_context before generation.
method_context.non_evidence_rule: AI output is experimental text and cannot become archive evidence.
method_context.refusal_rule: Refuse or request narrower context when required fields or claim support are absent.

Generated model text is not archive evidence; it is an experimental output judged against the evidence packet. If a query asks for a claim not supported by the packet, the correct behavior is to refuse or request narrower context.

notes.raw: Sergey Filippov PSE Russia 2012.jpg is an open-license Commons image record linked to Global / release gate expansion. 100th birth anniversary of the Soviet Russian film and theatre actor Sergey Filippov (1912–1990). The illustrated postal stationery envelope with commemorative stamp, Russia, 22 June 2012. Catalogue of PTC Marka No 229/кор-12, Michel No USo230. Envelope paper VBM (ВБМ) with watermarks “ПОЧТА РОССИИ”. Offset printing. Size of the envelope: 114×162 mm. Print run: 500,000. The commemorative non-denominated stamp “Letter A” (for domestic letters, weight 20g or less): the portrait of Sergey Filippov. The illustration: Sergey Filippov as Ippolit Matveyevich (Kisa) Vorobyaninov in the comedy film The  100th birth anniversary of the Soviet Russian film and theatre actor Sergey Filippov (1912–1990). notes.raw: Putzmühle im Pöbeltal 002.JPG is an open-license Commons image record linked to Global / release gate expansion. Putzmühle , first recorded in 1600 as one of three flour-mills of Schellerhau. 1618 "Christoph Wecks mill" with compulsory use for Schellerhau, Oberpöbel and Schönfeld. 1690–1698 stamp mill and tin-washings for a nearby tin-mine of the "Hilfe Gottes, Zwitterzeche am Pöbelberg" mining union. 1698–1912 flour-mill, owned by the naming Putze family. Destroyed by fire and re-erected in 1831. Temporarily saw-mill. 1912–1980 restaurant; 1970–1980 in state ownwership. Disused 1980–1998, then reconstructed and carried on as restaurant by Gommlich family. notes.raw: Putzmühle im Pöbeltal 002.JPG is an open-license Commons image record linked to Global / release gate expansion. Putzmühle , first recorded in 1600 as one of three flour-mills of Schellerhau. 1618 "Christoph Wecks mill" with compulsory use for Schellerhau, Oberpöbel and Schönfeld. 1690–1698 stamp mill and tin-washings for a nearby tin-mine of the "Hilfe Gottes, Zwitterzeche am Pöbelberg" mining union. 1698–1912 flour-mill, owned by the naming Putze family. Destroyed by fire and re-erected in 1831. Temporarily saw-mill. 1912–1980 restaurant; 1970–1980 in state ownwership. Disused 1980–1998, then reconstructed and carried on as restaurant by Gommlich family. The Town Hall, Market Place, Tamworth Built in 1701 by Thomas Guy, M.P. for the town. It is of mellow brick with arcades at ground level, large Jacobean windows and a steeply-pitched roof topped by a cupola. (The Shell Guide to England, 1970) It once housed the Butter Market and was used to store the towns fire engine. The rear was added in the 19th century. The Tourist Information Centre is located next to it. In front is the bronze statue of Sir Robert Peel, dating from 1852. notes.raw: Putzmühle im Pöbeltal 002.JPG is an open-license Commons image record linked to Global / release gate expansion. Putzmühle , first recorded in 1600 as one of three flour-mills of Schellerhau. 1618 "Christoph Wecks mill" with compulsory use for Schellerhau, Oberpöbel and Schönfeld. 1690–1698 stamp mill and tin-washings for a nearby tin-mine of the "Hilfe Gottes, Zwitterzeche am Pöbelberg" mining union. 1698–1912 flour-mill, owned by the naming Putze family. Destroyed by fire and re-erected in 1831. Temporarily saw-mill. 1912–1980 restaurant; 1970–1980 in state ownwership. Disused 1980–1998, then reconstructed and carried on as restaurant by Gommlich family. This leaf records Putzmühle im Pöbeltal 002.JPG as a re Putzmühle , first recorded in 1600 as one of three flour-mills of Schellerhau. 1618 "Christoph Wecks mill" with compulsory use for Schellerhau, Oberpöbel and Schönfeld. 1690–1698 stamp mill and tin-washings for a nearby tin-mine of the "Hilfe Gottes, Zwitterzeche am Pöbelberg" mining union. 1698–1912 flour-mill, owned by the naming Putze family. The Town Hall, Market Place, Tamworth Built in 1701 by Thomas Guy, M.P. for the town. It is of mellow brick with arcades at ground level, large Jacobean windows and a steeply-pitched roof topped by a cupola. (The Shell Guide to England, 1970) It once housed the Butter Market and was used to store the towns fire engine. The rear was added in the 19th century. The Tourist Information Centre is located next to it. In front is the bronze statue of Sir Robert Peel, dating from 1852. evidence_records.medium: 2 p. ; in-8; Nombre total de vues : 6

rights.label: IMG03: Gallica SRU metadata reports public-domain rights; IIIF image is source-hosted by BnF.

notes.raw: The archive keeps Monsieur, vous avez réclamé votre inscription... as a traceable Gallica visual/document record connected back to Gallica / BnF APIs. The item metadata gives date evidence: 1830; place evidence: France; form/medium: Gallica visual/document record. The source-side description adds this evidence: Appartient à l’ensemble documentaire : Centre1; Avec mode texte; 2 p.; in-8; Nombre total de vues : 6 Notice du catalogue : http://catalogue.bnf.fr/ark:/12148/cb36317544m; (Orléans). The project keeps this as an index-and-return surface: the page gives enough context to read the record while the source link remains the evidentiary home. Appartient à l’ensemble documentaire : Centre1; Avec mode texte; 2 p Appartient à l’ensemble documentaire : Centre1; Avec mode texte; 2 p.; in-8; Nombre total de vues : 6 Notice du catalogue : http://catalogue.bnf.fr/ark:/12148/cb36317544m; (Orléans) France (1824-1830, Charles X) Within the pre-1914 record set, this item belongs to the expansion of lithographic, typographic, periodical, advertising, and administrative print cultures that made modern graphic design legible before the profession was fully named. The record is read as graphic communication evidence: a designed surface whose metadata, source link, image state, and classification help connect it to broader design-history structure Folder membership is a filter assignment, not a claim of ownership or a closed historical interpretation. It is derived from source title, object type, medium, date, place, subjects, source institution, and review notes.

The Town Hall, Market Place, Tamworth Built in 1701 by Thomas Guy, M.P. for the town. It is of mellow brick with arcades at ground level, large Jacobean windows and a steeply-pitched roof topped by a cupola. (The Shell Guide to England, 1970) It once housed the Butter Market and was used to store the towns fire engine. The rear was added in the 19th century. The Tourist Information Centre is located next to it. In front is the bronze statue of Sir Robert Peel, dating from 1852. Putzmühle , first recorded in 1600 as one of three flour-mills of Schellerhau. 1618 "Christoph Wecks mill" with compulsory use for Schellerhau, Oberpöbel and Schönfeld. 1690–1698 stamp mill and tin-washings for a nearby tin-mine of the "Hilfe Gottes, Zwitterzeche am Pöbelberg" mining union. 1698–1912 flour-mill, owned by the naming Putze family. Destroyed by fire and re-erected in 1831. Temporarily saw-mill. 1912–1980 restaurant; 1970–1980 in state ownwership. Disused 1980–1998, then reconstructed and carried on as restaurant by Gommlich family. notes.raw: The Town Hall, Market Place, Tamworth - geograph.org.uk - 1741283.jpg is an open-license Commons image record linked to Global / release gate expansion. The Town Hall, Market Place, Tamworth Built in 1701 by Thomas Guy, M.P. for the town. It is of mellow brick with arcades at ground level, large Jacobean windows and a steeply-pitched roof topped by a cupola. (The Shell Guide to England, 1970) It once housed the Butter Market and was used to store the towns fire engine. The rear was added in the 19th century. notes.raw: Putzmühle im Pöbeltal 002.JPG is an open-license Commons image record linked to Global / release gate expansion. Putzmühle , first recorded in 1600 as one of three flour-mills of Schellerhau. 1618 "Christoph Wecks mill" with compulsory use for Schellerhau, Oberpöbel and Schönfeld. 1690–1698 stamp mill and tin-washings for a nearby tin-mine of the "Hilfe Gottes, Zwitterzeche am Pöbelberg" mining union. 1698–1912 flour-mill, owned by the naming Putze family. Destroyed by fire and re-erected in 1831. Temporarily saw-mill. 1912–1980 restaurant; 1970–1980 in state ownwership. Disused 1980–1998, then reconstructed and carried on as restaurant by Gommlich family. notes.raw: The Town Hall, Market Place, Tamworth - geograph.org.uk - 1741283.jpg is an open-license Commons image record linked to Global / release gate expansion. The Town Hall, Market Place, Tamworth Built in 1701 by Thomas Guy, M.P. for the town. It is of mellow brick with arcades at ground level, large Jacobean windows and a steeply-pitched roof topped by a cupola. (The Shell Guide to England, 1970) It once housed the Butter Market and was used to store the towns fire engine. The rear was added in the 19th century. The Tourist Information Centre is located next to it. In front is the bronze statue of Sir Robert Peel, dating from 1852. Former Tamworth MP and twice Prime Minister, Sir Robert delivered his famous Tamwo The Town Hall, Market Place, Tamworth Built in 1701 by Thomas Guy, M.P. for the town. notes.raw: The Town Hall, Market Place, Tamworth - geograph.org.uk - 1741283.jpg is an open-license Commons image record linked to Global / release gate expansion. The Town Hall, Market Place, Tamworth Built in 1701 by Thomas Guy, M.P. for the town. It is of mellow brick with arcades at ground level, large Jacobean windows and a steeply-pitched roof topped by a cupola. (The Shell Guide to England, 1970) It once housed the Butter Market and was used to store the towns fire engine. The rear was added in the 19th century. The Tourist Information Centre is located next to it. In front is the bronze statue of Sir Robert Peel, dating from 1852.
